subject: ap-calculus-ab
theme: Derivatives describe real-world rates of change — from the motion of a particle to the growth of a shadow — and L'Hôpital's Rule handles the special case where direct substitution yields an indeterminate form.

core_concepts.title: Position, Velocity, and Acceleration
core_concepts.detail: For a particle with position $s(t)$: velocity is $v(t) = s'(t)$, acceleration is $a(t) = v'(t) = s''(t)$, and speed is $|v(t)|$.

core_concepts.title: Speeding Up vs. Slowing Down
core_concepts.detail: A particle is speeding up when velocity and acceleration have the same sign; slowing down when they have opposite signs.

core_concepts.title: Linearization and Local Approximation
core_concepts.detail: Linearization: $L(x) = f(a) + f'(a)(x-a)$ is the tangent line at $x = a$ and gives a good approximation of $f(x)$ for $x$ near $a$ (local linear approximation).

core_concepts.title: L'Hopital's Rule
core_concepts.detail: L'Hôpital's Rule applies only when $\lim_{x \to c} f(x) = 0$ and $\lim_{x \to c} g(x) = 0$, or both approach $\pm \infty$; then $\lim \frac{f}{g} = \lim \frac{f'}{g'}$.

core_concepts.title: Related Rates Setup
core_concepts.detail: Related rates: use implicit differentiation with respect to time to connect rates like $\frac{dA}{dt}$ and $\frac{dr}{dt}$ through a geometric formula.

core_concepts.title: Related Rates Problem Strategy
core_concepts.detail: In related rates, identify all variables, write an equation relating them, differentiate both sides with respect to $t$, then substitute known values.

core_concepts.title: Units of the Derivative
core_concepts.detail: Units of the derivative: if $f$ has units of 'output units' and $x$ has units of 'input units,' then $f'(x)$ has units of 'output units per input unit.'

core_concepts.title: Tangent Line Error and Concavity
core_concepts.detail: The tangent line is an overestimate when the function is concave down ($f'' < 0$) and an underestimate when concave up ($f'' > 0$).

formulas.name: Position, Velocity, Acceleration
formulas.description: Velocity is the rate of change of position. Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity. Speed is the magnitude of velocity and is always non-negative. These three are connected by differentiation.

formulas.name: Linearization (Local Linear Approximation)
formulas.description: The tangent line to $f$ at $x = a$. For $x$ close to $a$, $L(x) \approx f(x)$. This is how you approximate $f(2.01)$ if you know $f(2)$ and $f'(2)$ exactly. It's precise enough for exam questions asking for 'approximate' values.

formulas.name: L'Hôpital's Rule
formulas.description: When a limit gives $0/0$ or $\infty/\infty$ after substitution, differentiate the numerator and denominator separately (not as a quotient!) and re-evaluate the limit. Verify the indeterminate form before applying — always check first.
formulas.example: $\lim_{x \to 0} \frac{\sin x}{x}$: this gives $0/0$, so apply L'Hôpital: $\lim_{x \to 0} \frac{\cos x}{1} = 1$.

formulas.name: Related Rates — Chain Rule Setup
formulas.description: Use the chain rule to connect different rates of change. Differentiate the relating formula with respect to time $t$. This is the template: express $V$ in terms of $r$, differentiate, substitute the given rate $\frac{dr}{dt}$.
formulas.example: Sphere: $V = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3$. Differentiating: $\frac{dV}{dt} = 4\pi r^2 \frac{dr}{dt}$. If $r = 5$ cm and $\frac{dr}{dt} = 2$ cm/s, then $\frac{dV}{dt} = 4\pi(25)(2) = 200\pi$ cm³/s.

formulas.name: Area of a Circle (Related Rates)
formulas.description: The area of a circle depends on radius $r$. When both change with time, differentiate the area formula with respect to $t$ using the chain rule. This is the most common related-rates geometric formula.
formulas.example: If a circle's radius grows at 3 cm/s and $r = 4$ cm, then $\frac{dA}{dt} = 2\pi(4)(3) = 24\pi$ cm²/s.

exam_tip: On the AP exam, L'Hôpital's Rule is frequently misapplied — you MUST verify the indeterminate form first (show that both numerator and denominator $\to 0$ or both $\to \infty$) before differentiating; partial credit is lost if this step is skipped.
